25 - Why Prospects Dont Trust You (And How to Fix It Fast) - YouTube.mp4

Remember, you're committed to mastery. That's why you're here, right? Okay. So write this down. This is a writer downer. So verbal cues are basically sound. Sounds out of your mouth while the prospect's talking. So when you ask a question and they're talking to show that you're actually listening to them, that you're present in the situation. Cause, if you've ever been on a phone call and you start telling somebody like a deep problem that you have, and two minutes later, you don't hear anything on the other end, the whole time you're talking and you say, 'Hello, are you still there?' It's because they don't know how to use verbal cues to show that they're present in the conversation.
Now, verbal cues, we also use them to to bridge from question to question. This is extremely important. So bridging means I'm transitioning from question to question. I don't wanna ask a question. Answer, then I sit back and say, 'Okay, all right, gotcha, gotcha.' John, I'm curious, let me ask you, like, that sounds scripted, sounds like a sales question, okay? So I'm going to show you how to bridge from question to question using verbal cues. So examples of verbal cues might be like, 'ah,' or 'hmm,' or 'right,' or 'okay.' Or, 'oh,' interesting. Or, 'really.' Or, 'oh,' I see. Okay. There's just eight examples. Now, you're not going to use a verbal cue every two or three seconds because that's going to sound weird.
But like, let's, I would even say seven seconds. Let's say every 10 to maybe 20 seconds of a conversation. Uh-huh. Ah. Okay. Really? I'm kind of exaggerating that. Probably 10 to 20 seconds before I use a verbal cue. That's what I mean by that. Now, bridging. This is why this is so important, because if you don't bridge from question to question, you're going to sound scripted. And especially when you're asking situation questions, if you don't use verbal cues, your prospect is going to feel like you're interrogating them because the questions you ask in most of situation questions are more factual-based questions. You're finding out the fact. Okay, last one or two of our situation questions. We're going to start to see doubt, but you don't want to sound scripted.
All right, so you don't want to say, at the end of the when the prospect answers, like, 'okay, cool,' or 'right,' 'right,' 'right,' or 'okay, awesome, awesome, awesome.' I see a lot of salespeople do that. Prospect dancers, awesome, awesome. Let me ask you, it just sounds unnatural. You want to make the prospect feel like they are talking to their best friend. And a best friend would probably not say, at the end of them, saying, 'something back to you,' like, 'okay, cool, right, gotcha, let me ask you Jane.' That would sound weird. You wouldn't say that to a friend. And you want them to feel like you're their best friend. So you have to bridge quickly. Okay, all right. So that's verbal cues. Bridge.
